segments.text: Hello everyone, today we're going to talk about business models and pricing strategies.
This is our Safe Harbor statement.
And just to introduce ourselves, my name is Emil Cafuri, I'm the senior director for platform
engineering and the at the focused on the developer ecosystem.
And with me is my colleague, Erica Beffert, who's the director of product management,
also for the developer ecosystem.
So let's start with choosing the right business model.
You've built something great.
Now what do you do?
If you've been to any of my other classes, you'll know I'm a big fan of starting with the basics,
the product market fit.
The product market fit is really simple.
It's really, do you have the right product for the right market at the right price delivered
in the right way so that your customers will pay for it and tell others about it?
Even though this is really simple, each one of these items needs to be tested and confirmed
to make sure that you have product market fit and are able to go through that hypergrowth
cycle.
Let me give you an example of the same product that can have different outcomes once you've
got the product market fit, right?
Here's a product that is a structural inspection and tracking tool.
It is a desktop application sold for $99.
And the engineers that are sitting at their desk using this tool absolutely love it.
Nobody ever pushed back on the price because it was saving over $1,000 a month in labor
costs.
But the field inspectors that were on the job site weren't able to use it.
They don't always have internet access and they don't have a desktop application with
them because they're using a mobile application or a tablet.
So once the developers of the product realized that they had reached a cap for their sales,
they went back and looked at what was holding the product back.
They rebuilt it mobile first with offline capable and it would sink once it connected
to the internet.
It was basically the same product but it was delivered in a different way.
And then they also reassessed the price and they turned it into a $50 a month subscription.
And now with both of these things adjusted and fixed, they started to see significant
growth in the product because now it was delivered in the right way and it was properly
priced based on the value that was being delivered.
And so it's really important to be able to pick these right tools and be able to use all
of these bits of information that you're getting from your customers.
So there are basically three decisions that make up your model.
There's the distribution that you use, there's the pricing that you use and there's the
value that you're delivering and does it align with the expectations of your customer.
Let's first jump into the delivery model and look at what your options are and how they
can be used.
So the first option is the one that most of us are familiar with.
It's basically that option of downloading something, whether it's to your desktop, your
Mac, your PC or downloading it to your phone or tablet.
And there's certainly advantages in that most people are used to using that.
But it does create a lot of overhead, there's a lot of different types of hardware.
And so now you've got to make sure the application that you've built works on everyone's hardware.
And so that is certainly one way to go.
That is the way almost every rev it, AutoCAD, inventor application is delivered to the customers.
The other option completely at the other end is a SaaS solution, basically a web application
that is delivered in the same way effectively to everybody.
And the advantage of course is that you're building this once and everyone can take advantage
of it and use your product.
On the other hand, you're also incurring a lot of costs that you wouldn't be incurring
if you're using the downloader on-prem solution in that you're going to be incurring all
of the hardware costs and all of the maintenance of this product.
So certainly understanding what the advantages and disadvantages are.
And then there's a hybrid solution where it may be a same product, but it's
in delivered in different ways or potentially a on-prem or desktop product that is cloud connected
and can have extra value delivered once it is connected to the cloud.
And then of course the actual way that it's distributed is also really important.
Are you selling or providing this product through just your website, in which case people
need to be able to find your website, to be able to find your product?
Or are you going to leverage a third-party marketplace like Autodesk's Design and Make Marketplace?
Once you do that, it's important to understand all of the cost and all of the value that you're
getting from it.
For example, these public marketplaces may have a huge amount of marketing budget and a huge
amount of traffic that goes through them.
And so even though it may be more costly than just delivering it on your website, it can
actually provide you with a much larger distribution.
Over here it's also really important to think about the naming for your product because
if you put your company's name on it that could have positive or negative impact on the
product.
So think long and hard about your brand and whether you want to build your products brand
or your company's brand with your product.
Just as an example, let's say you are a channel partner and Autodesk channel partner in
France and you've built an amazing application.
If you put your name on it, it may make it difficult for a channel partner in Italy,
for example, to want to sell your product because they don't want to elevate your company
brand.
Similarly, if you're an engineering company in Italy and you put your name on that product
that you're selling, your app that you're selling, you may find out that engineering
companies and other parts of the world may also not want to buy or use your product because
they don't want to be supporting a competitor.
So picking that name is really important.
Keep that in mind.
So out this presentation, I have a number of worksheets for you that you'll also be able
to access that will allow you to work through some of these questions and try and figure
out what the right options are for you.
Now moving on to the pricing model.
Again, there's many different options and once we get to Erica's presentation, she's
going to dig into how these things relate to your connection with Autodesk and Autodesk
platform services and the other Autodesk portfolio products.
So the most classic way of charging someone for your product is that one time purchase
or what's called a perpetual license.
The agreement you basically have with your users is you give me your money.
I give you the product, we go in different directions and never bother each other again.
It's not exactly the world's best customer experience and it really leaves a lot undone.
And so there's other ways and sometimes better ways to sell.
But keep in mind that almost every app that's sold on the Apple and Google App Store is
sold in this way.
So there's certainly nothing wrong with this, but there's sometimes better options for
more complex products.
The next one is a usage based or consumption based pricing model, which basically allows
someone to use it and the more they use it, the more they're paying you for it.
And that can align your value with the price very directly.
But a lot of times people don't want to be burdened by having to worry about how much
they use the product and how much it's going to cost them.
The next option is a subscription.
And this is sort of where you see a lot of products moving to today, where the agreement
is I keep providing you with the product as it continues to improve and you the customer
will continue to pay me for it.
And then there's the premium that can be overlaid on any of these options, which is a certain
amount of usage or a certain level of capabilities are provided for free.
And if the user likes it and wants more of that, then they would start to pay for it.
And there's always the option to be able to tear your pricing, sort of a good, better
best model.
And in this case, what you can do is make sure that everyone has access based on their
willingness to pay.
So a single user, a less sophisticated user, would be able to go with that good pricing,
basic capabilities.
The better pricing allows capabilities like collaboration and reporting, sort of more
advanced capabilities.
And then that best pricing would be for enterprise users, integrating with single sign-on, providing
administrator capabilities and service level agreements.
And then of course, all of these can be blended together in sort of a hybrid pricing.
What's really important though is to make sure that you start very simple with one model,
the thing that you think will be the most accessible to your customer.
And then layer on complexity as you learn more about the way that your customer is willing
to pay for your product.
And again, I have a worksheet for you on this one.
The next thing is to really explore the value that you are delivering and how you want
people to pay for it.
There's always the simple cost plus model.
Basically you look at what the cost was for you to develop the product and the ongoing
maintenance, amortize that for example over five years, add a margin to it and that's
your price.
On the other hand, what we recommend is that you look at value pricing.
And the way that works is you start off for example with a price that is about 10% of
what the customer is saving by using your product.
Then you make sure you have a good margin after you've considered your labor cost and your
ongoing maintenance cost and infrastructure cost.
If the price is too high for your customers to buy it, they may just walk away and you
know that you may not have a viable product if it's price too high or if when you add
all of your expenses, you simply can't get the margin that you want.
So that's an indicator that maybe this is the wrong product, the wrong price delivered
in the wrong way to the wrong people.
On the other hand, if the price is too low, you always have room to be able to raise
your price.
So that is not necessarily a problem.
It allows you to seed the market as you learn more about your customer.
How do you explore the willingness to pay?
What you don't want to do is basically ask people how much do you want to pay for this
thing.
First of all, they may low-ball you.
One of all, they may actually not know what the value of this product is to them.
So ask them questions that helps both of you understand the value that you're delivering.
How did you solve this today?
What does it cost your team?
What happens when it goes wrong?
All of these things will help you get a better understanding of the value of the product.
Also, make sure that you see if people are pushing back on the price.
If they're not, it could simply be too cheap.
The next thing is to explore, instead of guessing, run experiments, iterate, think like lean
methodology, try something, see how customers react, and based on that feedback, be able
to refine your pricing and refine your business model.
Lightweight experiments are always really, really valuable.
So like the proverbial iceberg, you want to start with digging deeper down.
At the top, the stuff that's above the surface, it's really that surface level pricing questions.
But as you dig deeper down below the waterline, you'll start to really understand the value,
the time save, the productivity, the risk avoided, etc.
So don't look at that bit on top, just the price, think about all the value you're delivering.
So going back to the product market fit.
Remember, it's the right product for the right market at the right price delivered in
the right way for customers to pay for it and just as importantly to tell others about
it.
So going back to that example, just changing the way the product is delivered and the
pricing can make a big difference for how your product will grow.
And again, I have a worksheet for you on this.
So just to recap before I hand it off to Erica, the four takeaways are think lean, start
simple, learn fast, evolve deliberately.
Price based on value, not cost.
Your delivery model is really important.
So be very, very deliberate about that and experiment, don't guess.
And with that, I'm going to hand it off to Erica.
For this talk, we are assuming that most of you are building applications that are using
APS APIs and that the cost of using those APIs is one factor that you will consider as
you're going through the exercises that a meal has outlined.
So I want to talk now about changes to the APS business model that you should be aware
of and some features and that we're currently working on that will be coming in the next
year.
In December, we made several key improvements to the APS business model.
First of all, we switched from the limited 90 day trials to an APS free tier, which gives
everyone that signs up ongoing monthly access to all of our APIs with a free but useful
amount of usage every month.
And second, we increased the different ways that you're able to pay for those of our
paid APIs.
Not only can you purchase and flex tokens in advance to pay for usage, but you can also
in select markets sign up for pay as you go and get billed every month for for your usage.
This capability is available currently in certain markets and we will continue to expand
that.
In May, we're introducing an additional way to get access to APS APIs that's called included
usage for companies using APS for internal use.
Access subscriptions may now include monthly API usage capacity.
Products will typically include APIs related to their product and I'll go through an example
here on the next slide to illustrate this.
The amount of monthly capacity scales with the number of subscriptions purchased and
included usage is designed to work seamlessly with a prepaid or pay as you go to ensure that
you have ongoing access to your APIs.
If your monthly capacity for any API is ever used up, tokens or pay as you go will seamlessly
kick in to cover that usage and ensure that service on nudity.
The first two APIs, which we will offer using this model are AEC data model and the manufacturing
data model.
So I want to go through here through a brief description and I want to highlight that
on the right hand side what you're seeing is the rate sheet for the amount of usage
that's included with different products subscriptions.
This is available on our blog, on a recent announcement.
If you go to APS.autodesk.com and go to our community section and click on our blog, you'll
see it right there at the top.
You can get a bigger view of it and you can read more about the details of these coming
capabilities.
So let's say that I purchased five subscriptions to Autodesk build.
Autodesk build now has included with it a hundred megabytes per subscription of granular
data download.
So this means when I just to emphasize different between granular data and file data, you're
not downloading entire files that have a very big size but you're downloading all of
the window dimensions from a model or all of the number of doors.
If you have five subscriptions, that means every month you will get access to 500 megabytes
of the ability to download granular data out of that AEC data model and then any of the
APS applications which you have built on the N or N or N or developer hub on that same
team will be able to use that capacity.
One of the things I do want to highlight is that this capacity included with products
subscriptions is intended for non-commercial use which means you would spend it on an application
that you built internally.
But if you're an ISD, that included use is not intended for you to spend on running one
of your commercial applications.
But we've got capabilities coming that will help you take advantage of the included
usage being offered to your customers.
Customers can spend their included usage on the apps that they build or the apps that
they acquire from partners such as yourself.
It's the ISV or you as a company's choice who is going to pay for that APS usage and
what I want to talk a little bit now is how that works and how that relates to the, you
sort of make a choice between who's going to pay based on the decisions that you're making
about which business model or what's pricing model you want to support for your application.
So first of all, this choice is available to all of you today.
You as the ISV who's running the application calling APS APIs can choose today to pay for
all of the APS usage that is incurred by those applications and bakelack into the cost
of your application whether that's a monthly subscription or other pricing model.
You can also choose when a customer wants to sign up for your product to ask your customer
to create a developer hub and an APS app, generate that client ID and then share it with you
and you can save that in your application and then you're using that when you make the
API calls and the customer pays for that usage.
We do have customer, you know, ISV's and customers doing that now.
But the feedback that we've got from both of them is that while this makes this method
possible, it's not that easy.
It's not a good user experience for customers or for ISVs and we've taken that feedback
and we'll be working to improve that workflow.
So this is a capability that we're calling pass through and what it means is that when
we release this capability, the ISV will still be able to choose to pay for all the API
usage or they can choose to have the customer pay it and how this works is you can have
multiple different applications and you can decide to have some of those applications where
you're going to pay for the usage and then those others you designate as customer pays.
When the customer wants to sign up for your application, they agree to pay for that usage
and once that workflow is complete, then the customer can now use that included usage that came
with our products subscription to pay for the API usage of your application and then if it needs
more than the monthly allotment they would pay with tokens or with pay as you go.
The goal of pass through is to give both the customer and the ISV visibility into and control over
usage to make it easier to budget and prepare for the operating costs of running different applications.
As I mentioned, this is the capability that we're currently working on and if you were interested
in joining our feature sponsorship program and shaping the design and development of this capability,
please reach out to me and we'll see if that ends up being a good fit for both of us.
So how does this relate back to the choices that you'll be making as you go through your pricing model?
I put up the six different proposed models that a meal talked through at the beginning of his
presentation. I've kind of put them on a spectrum here and so as you're going through that exercise
and deciding which pricing model is going to make the most sense for your application, you can kind
of think about the spectrum where if you're going with a tiered model or a subscription model where
you want to give your customer that fixed cost and your customer is communicated to you that they
want to have an ongoing same amount of cost and you've built in a healthy margin there. Then for
subscription and tiered, it really makes more sense to pay for APS directly yourself and then maintain
that direct relationship between you and the customer. They don't have to set up something with us
and something with you. It's just it's all you. On the other hand, if for example you are putting a
free application into the app store or to the marketplace and or you're asking for one time payment
on the application, those are business model types where it would make sense if there's any APS
costs associated with that to sign up for a pass through and have that application be sort of a
one-time or free cost to the customer with the ongoing usage going to to a pass through.
If you're doing hybrid or usage based, again, this is one of those where you'll want to try some
experiments. You can start using some of the the you start your application out maybe in the
free tier, run a couple of months of that application where you're paying for that usage and observe
how customers are using your application and whether it's going to make more sense for their usage
patterns, the margin that you're that you're looking to achieve from that application and the the
price instruction you want to achieve, whether you want to simply pay for that usage yourself
or whether you want to pass that through to customers. The other thing that you'll want to think
about and consider is do your customers have a lot of auto desk products? Look at those rate
sheets and try to understand the amount of different APIs that amount of monthly usage that they're
going to have access to and certainly think about how to make your your applications use of the
API's efficient so that that is attractive to the customers because now sort of like fuel efficiency
that would be something that a customer would look at if they were considering whether or not to
sign up for pass through. All right so with all of that being said we are constantly
interested in hearing from you and hearing your feedback and so whether you sign up for the
feature sponsorship program for this particular feature or not we definitely welcome you to join
the developer Vanguard program. This is the place that many of the product teams at auto desk go to
when they are interested in talking to customers about an API that they're building or feature that
they're building to really make sure that they get that that customer feedback and so if you
are interested in a particular one of either the APIs or other programs that are listed here
definitely sign up but even if you're just in general want to express interest of other topics
you'd like to be called on for you can register that as well and make that connection with us.
Thank you.
